players.name: Rachaad White
players.performance_evidence: Served as Tampa Bay's lead back in 2023 with 990 rushing yards and 49 receptions, showing three-down capability. Production declined in 2024 as Austin Ekeler took passing-down work; rushed for roughly 600 yards on a lower YPC (~3.8). Signed with Washington as a complementary back behind Brian Robinson Jr. Solid pass protector and receiver out of the backfield but lacks explosive play ability.

trade_value.reasoning: Final year of a cheap rookie deal with declining production and a complementary role. Useful RB2 but expiring contract and replacement-level production limit value to a late-round pick swap at best.

players.availability_reasoning: Signed as a depth/complementary piece behind Brian Robinson Jr. Washington has no long-term commitment. On an expiring deal with minimal dead cap, easily movable if a contender needs RB depth midseason.

players.name: Treylon Burks
players.performance_evidence: Has largely failed to live up to 1st-round pedigree. In three seasons with Tennessee, never exceeded 500 receiving yards in a season. Posted 32 catches for 391 yards and 2 TDs in 2024. Conditioning and route-running have been consistent concerns. Flashed some ability as a big-slot/gadget player but has not developed into a reliable starter. Washington acquired him hoping a fresh start unlocks his physical tools (6'2, 225 lbs, 4.55 speed).

trade_value.reasoning: Former 1st-round pick on a cheap remaining deal provides some lottery-ticket value, but consistent underperformance limits trade return to a 6th-round pick equivalent at best. The upside of his physical profile and remaining team control at low cost provides marginal value.

players.availability_reasoning: Washington likely acquired Burks at low cost as a reclamation project. If he doesn't show progress in camp, he's easily movable or cuttable. Low dead cap makes the move painless. Not a core piece of the roster.
players.injury_notes: Struggled with conditioning issues as a rookie, missed time early in career. Had an asthma-related episode during 2022 training camp.
players.trade_buzz: Tennessee traded him away, signaling they had given up on his development. Could be a camp cut candidate if he doesn't show improvement.

players.name: John Bates
players.performance_evidence: Primarily an inline blocking tight end who provides value in the run game. Has never exceeded 25 receptions or 250 receiving yards in a season. Caught 17 passes for 180 yards and 1 TD in 2024. Washington values him for his blocking ability, which earned him a modest extension. PFF grades him as an above-average run blocker but well below average as a receiver.

trade_value.reasoning: Blocking tight ends have minimal trade value. Provides scheme-specific value as a TE2/run-game contributor but would not generate meaningful draft capital in a trade. Worth a 7th-round pick at most.

players.availability_reasoning: Solid contributor but ultimately a role player. If a run-heavy team came calling with a late-round pick, Washington could replace his production. Not being actively shopped but not someone they'd turn down fair compensation for.

players.name: Chris Paul
players.performance_evidence: Emerged as a surprise starter on Washington's offensive line after going undrafted out of Tulsa. Earned the starting LG job mid-season in 2024 and held it through the playoffs. Allowed some pressures but showed solid anchor in the run game. PFF graded him around 60-63, indicating a below-average to average starter. Young and ascending, with room for development under a coaching staff that has been excellent at developing offensive linemen.

trade_value.reasoning: Young starting guard on a minimum-level deal provides excellent cost-controlled value. However, he's only had one partial season as a starter and UDFA pedigree limits perceived value. Worth a 5th-round pick equivalent — the cheap contract and youth add value beyond his current play level.

players.availability_reasoning: Washington invested coaching capital in developing Paul and he's on a dirt-cheap deal. They wouldn't give him away, but if someone offered a 4th-round pick for a player they got for free, the math might work. Not being shopped — he's their starting LG.

players.name: Tim Settle Jr.
players.performance_evidence: Returned to Washington after stints with the Commanders (originally) and Bills. Solid interior run defender who also generates some pass rush — posted 4.5 sacks in 2023 with Buffalo and 3 sacks in 2024. PFF grades consistently in the 65-70 range. Not a game-wrecker but a dependable, versatile defensive tackle who can play nose or 3-technique in Washington's scheme. Above-average run defender.

trade_value.reasoning: Quality starting DT on a reasonable one-year deal. Expiring contract limits value significantly — acquiring team gets one year of above-average DT play. Worth a 5th-round pick to a contender needing interior DL help at the deadline.

players.availability_reasoning: Expiring contract with zero dead cap makes him a natural trade deadline candidate if Washington falls out of contention. Even if competing, he's a veteran on a short deal who could net draft capital. Depends entirely on the team's record by October.

players.name: Javon Kinlaw
players.performance_evidence: Former top-15 pick whose career has been severely hampered by a degenerative knee condition. Has played in only about 50 games across 5 NFL seasons. When healthy, flashes the disruptive interior ability that made him a 1st-round pick — posted 2.5 sacks and 24 pressures in 2023 with the Jets. However, durability is a constant question. In 2024/2025, served as a rotational piece whose snap counts were carefully managed. Below-average run defender but can collapse the pocket when fresh.

trade_value.reasoning: Former 1st-round talent on a cheap one-year deal, but chronic knee issues make him a significant injury risk. Expiring contract and health concerns limit value to a late-round pick. A team looking for a DT rental with upside might offer a 6th.

players.availability_reasoning: Expiring deal, zero dead cap, and Washington has Settle and other DL depth. Kinlaw is the definition of a movable piece — low cost to acquire, potential upside for a contender willing to manage his knee. Washington would take a late-round pick and move on.
players.injury_notes: Degenerative knee condition (arthritic knee) that has plagued his entire career. Required multiple procedures. Snap counts must be managed. This is the defining factor in his trade value and career trajectory.

players.name: K'Lavon Chaisson
players.performance_evidence: One of the biggest first-round busts of the 2020 class. Never developed a consistent pass rush in Jacksonville — totaled only 4 career sacks in 4 seasons with the Jaguars. Has bounced around since, landing in Washington as a rotational OLB/edge setter. Shows some utility in run defense and pursuit but offers almost nothing as a pass rusher. In 2024, recorded 1.5 sacks in a limited role. Athletic traits (4.53 speed at 254 lbs) remain intriguing but have never translated to NFL production.

trade_value.reasoning: Former 1st-round pick but 5+ years of underwhelming production have erased any draft pedigree value. Rotational edge player on a cheap expiring deal is worth a conditional 7th at best. No team is paying meaningful capital for a player Jacksonville gave up on.

players.availability_reasoning: Depth edge rusher on an expiring deal with zero guaranteed money. Washington has no long-term investment in Chaisson. If any team wants to take a flyer on his athleticism, Washington would happily take a late-round pick or swap. Could also be a camp cut candidate depending on roster competition.
